overrides.reason: Lost describes El Patron as a responsive, forgiving performance shortboard scaled for larger or older surfers, not a step-up or fish.
Lost explicitly calls Big Rig Driver a high-volume Pro-Formance Series shortboard for larger or aging shortboard riders.
The official description identifies the model as the grom-sized Driver 3.0 for top-tier performance surfing.
Lost presents RNF 96 as the modern performance continuation of the Round Nose Fish rather than a purely traditional fish.
Pyzel designed Ghost Swallow for high-performance surfing in hollow, powerful reef, beach and point-break waves from about four feet.
Pyzel positions Power Tiger between Red Tiger and Ghost, with refined rails, added curve and hold for stronger, more powerful surf.
This is the official grom-sized Power Tiger and retains the parent model's performance-shortboard identity.
Firewire explicitly describes Great White Twin as a high-performance groveller with hybrid paddle support and shortboard rail performance.
The official 6'8 to 7'4 design is a performance mid length using a twinzer arrangement, not a step-up.
Album describes Moonstone as a dynamic mid length evolved from a stretched fish platform, with the longer design role remaining primary.
Album explicitly calls Townsend a hybrid mid length and step-up with egg-like paddle and appearance; the mid-length identity is primary.
Channel Islands designed Goldie to maximise barrel time in steep, heavy waves and identifies it as a proven heavy-wave barrel board.
Channel Islands explicitly introduces Happy as a performance shortboard; tube capability is part of its range, not a step-up identity.
The official design retains performance-board intent while adding forward volume for easier paddling and wave entry.
DHD calls MF Twin a modern high-performance twin and explicitly states that it is not a retro fish.
Firewire presents Greedy Beaver as a crossover with mid-length glide and shortboard manoeuvrability; longboard outline influence is secondary.
Firewire explicitly combines a step-up twin with a performance mid length; its planing-hull mid-length platform remains the public identity.
Firewire calls Xtra Cado a mid-length evolution of Machadocado; longboard references describe glide rather than identity.
Lost explicitly says Cali Twin Pin is not a true step-up and positions it as a performance bridge between fish and shortboard.
Lost calls Hydra the ultimate minuscule-wave weapon; fish design influences support its groveller identity rather than replace it.
Lost identifies Sub Driver 2.0 as a small-wave competition and pro-formance shortboard that is accessible enough for everyday surfers.
Pyzel develops Astro Pop from Astro Glider as a short, fast performance fish; everyday-shortboard wording only describes comparative sizing.
Ghost Pro is the John John Florence competition-derived Ghost; broad usability does not change its performance-shortboard identity.
Ghost XL retains Ghost's quality-wave performance design with fuller dimensions; one-board-quiver language describes versatility, not identity.
This official Gremlin softboard keeps the Gremlin small-wave performance role; longboard wording describes rider progression only.
Rusty explicitly calls Hatchet a groveller with high-performance characteristics; its semi-fishy outline is a design influence.
Rusty positions Miso as a fast performance-oriented groveller with fish influence and broader range than a traditional fish.
Rusty explicitly describes Panda as an HPSB for heavier surfers, with performance rocker, rails and vertical-turn intent.
Rusty explicitly identifies Slayer 2 as a modern step-up and recommends riding it longer than a daily performance shortboard.
